query: Could I be excluded from my studies for using AI in an exam when it wasn't allowed?

queries: Could I be excluded from my studies for using AI in an exam when it wasn't allowed?

retrieved.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
heading_path: This is usually prohibited:
retrieved.text:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

retrieved.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
retrieved.text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

retrieved.title: AI in your Studies: What is permitted?
retrieved.text:
AI can provide support at various points throughout your degree programme, such as idea structuring, understanding complex content and text revision. However, there are clear guidelines in place regarding the application and extent to which AI can be utilised in degree programmes.

The University of Vienna supports the responsible use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes, provided that such use is transparent, complies with regulations, upholds the [principles of academic integrity](https://backend.univie.ac.at/?id=225768), and that it supports you in achieving the intended learning outcomes.

Whether and how the use of AI is permitted always depends on the specific context. In case of doubt, the following applies: AI is not permitted unless clear permission has been granted.

[### Basic framework](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/)

[### What is permitted? And what is not?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/what-is-permitted-and-what-is-not/)

[### Data protection and copyright](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/)

[## Things AI is not recommended for](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/things-ai-is-not-recommended-for/)

[### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/)

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
retrieved.text:
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Always follow the teacher's instructions!
retrieved.text: In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

retrieved.title: Using AI in Your Studies
retrieved.text: Imagine you are tasked with writing an academic paper. You are aware of the importance of a clear research question, thorough research and a coherent line of argument. Yet instead of doing everything yourself, there is a new option: AI-supported tools can suggest literature, generate outlines, draft opening sentences and improve your writing style. Artificial intelligence has long become part of everyday student life. At the same time, its use in degree programmes raises important questions: What is permitted? Where are the boundaries? And what do students need to be aware of? On these pages, you will find concise and reliable information on the responsible use of AI in degree programmes at the University of Vienna. You will learn under which conditions AI may be used, which specific provisions apply to examinations and what requires particular attention when using AI. The content is based on the current regulations of the University of Vienna and is intended for students of all disciplines. The aim is to support a responsible, transparent and reflective use of AI. [### What Is Artificial Intelligence?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/) [### AI in Your Studies: What Is Permitted?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/) [### Academic work and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
retrieved.text:
AI can provide useful support during studies and exam preparation, for example by structuring the learning process, revision, feedback or self-assessment.

However, it is crucial to note that whether and how AI is permitted in examinations is determined by the respective lecturers. Students bear full responsibility for the independence, accuracy and transparency of their work. AI does not replace independent learning or engagement with the subject matter.

#

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
heading_path: Learning outcomes and independence
In short:
retrieved.text:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

retrieved.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
retrieved.text: - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

retrieved.title: Things AI is not recommended for
retrieved.text:
While AI has demonstrated remarkable capabilities, there are areas where its use is not recommended, as it could potentially compromise the autonomy of your scientific work or the integrity of your learning processes.

AI-generated summaries can provide an overview, but it is important to remember that they do not replace your own engagement with the material. Please note that AI-generated summaries are often also inadequate in terms of content, as they may omit essential aspects or distort statements from the original.

Recommendation: Engage with the topic yourself first or read the texts and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions, for example to clarify aspects you have not understood.

Tip: Do not use AI as a substitute for reading, but as a reading coach.

AI can support you in reading academic texts actively, critically and in a structured manner. For instance, it can provide guiding questions, suggestions for highlighting central arguments, or explanations of individual terms. It is recommended that you read the text yourself first and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions. This will help you to close gaps in understanding or to better contextualise argumentative structures (see tips on [prompting](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)).

Further information: [AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
retrieved.text: is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your studies, it is therefore important to use AI with restraint and to first develop your own learning strategies.

retrieved.title: Legal requirements
retrieved.text: In addition to the requirements arising from the learning outcomes [Lernzielen](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/#c1268367), the basic [rules of good scientific practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements of the respective teacher, regulations on data protection and copyright are also relevant when dealing with AI.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
retrieved.text: The use of AI tools can support the learning process, but it also presents challenges. In order to use AI sensibly and responsibly, it is important to consider a few fundamental aspects. - Due to its [functionality](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268970), AI may produce incorrect, incomplete or imprecise information, even if it sounds plausible. Therefore, it is advisable to always compare responses with your own materials. - Subject-specific terminology may be used differently depending on the discipline. When asked to define technical terms, AI generally draws on the meaning most frequently represented in its training data. This may result in definitions that do not correspond to the specific discipline or that lack subject-specific nuances. Only adopt definitions or explanations if they align with the academic requirements of your course. - AI-supported learning tools can heavily structure the learning process, for example through predefined learning paths, automatic revision or recommended content. While this can be helpful, it also entails the risk that your learning process becomes overly externally directed: learning is individual and not every recommendation generated by AI suits your personal learning style. Therefore, consciously set your own priorities. - A common risk factor is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: AI in exam preparation
retrieved.text:
AI tools can support exam preparation when used in a targeted and reflective manner. They are not a substitute for independent learning, but they can help to structure, deepen and reflect on the learning process.

AI tools can be particularly helpful in organising and supporting the learning process. This includes, among other things:

- Reflection on your own time management
- Support in organising learning activities and setting priorities
- Creating and adapting study and revision plans
- Motivation through structured learning steps or gamification elements
- Revising content, for example through questions or study plans on specific topics
- Gaining an overview of key terms, concepts or subject areas

This means that AI can help to structure the learning process, but it does not replace the need to engage with the subject matter.

AI tools can support learning in various ways, especially when used specifically for organisation, repetition, feedback and self-assessment. The following examples show typical usage scenarios that students can usefully integrate into their exam preparation.

retrieved.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
retrieved.text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

retrieved.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
retrieved.text: General information - Do not use generative AI for research - Do not trust AI-generated content; check everything - Write specific prompts with a clear task, context, and details on format and style - Do not rely on AI-generated summaries - Process AI output further - For each course, check the information in u:find, on Moodle, and information on specific assignments and examinations, and clarify any open questions about the use of AI with the respective lecturer Privacy Policy & Terms of Use - Do not enter sensitive or confidential data into AI tools - Anonymise or avoid entering personal data. - Obtain the express consent of third parties before processing their data in an AI. - Ensure that the data you enter is not used for AI training - Only use GDPR-compliant AI tools that do not store data on servers outside the EU. Copyright - Check whether AI-generated texts are based on copyrighted content and cite the authors appropriately - Ensure that copyrighted texts are reproduced correctly. - AI does not have copyright to its own output. Nevertheless, you may not pass off the content as your own. - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
retrieved.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

retrieved.title: Using AI in Your Studies
retrieved.text: and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was used in particular to shorten extensive texts and to simplify language, making content easier to understand and more accessible. The AI was used as a supporting tool, while content responsibility and quality review remained with the authors. The use of AI is based on the [Guidelines on AI in Studies and Teaching](https://doi.org/10.25365/phaidra.544), which aim to ensure a responsible and transparent use of AI applications in an academic context.

Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

reranked.title: Things AI is not recommended for
reranked.text:
While AI has demonstrated remarkable capabilities, there are areas where its use is not recommended, as it could potentially compromise the autonomy of your scientific work or the integrity of your learning processes.

AI-generated summaries can provide an overview, but it is important to remember that they do not replace your own engagement with the material. Please note that AI-generated summaries are often also inadequate in terms of content, as they may omit essential aspects or distort statements from the original.

Recommendation: Engage with the topic yourself first or read the texts and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions, for example to clarify aspects you have not understood.

Tip: Do not use AI as a substitute for reading, but as a reading coach.

AI can support you in reading academic texts actively, critically and in a structured manner. For instance, it can provide guiding questions, suggestions for highlighting central arguments, or explanations of individual terms. It is recommended that you read the text yourself first and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions. This will help you to close gaps in understanding or to better contextualise argumentative structures (see tips on [prompting](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)).

Further information: [AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
reranked.text: is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your studies, it is therefore important to use AI with restraint and to first develop your own learning strategies.

reranked.title: Legal requirements
reranked.text: In addition to the requirements arising from the learning outcomes [Lernzielen](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/#c1268367), the basic [rules of good scientific practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements of the respective teacher, regulations on data protection and copyright are also relevant when dealing with AI.

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
reranked.text: The use of AI tools can support the learning process, but it also presents challenges. In order to use AI sensibly and responsibly, it is important to consider a few fundamental aspects. - Due to its [functionality](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268970), AI may produce incorrect, incomplete or imprecise information, even if it sounds plausible. Therefore, it is advisable to always compare responses with your own materials. - Subject-specific terminology may be used differently depending on the discipline. When asked to define technical terms, AI generally draws on the meaning most frequently represented in its training data. This may result in definitions that do not correspond to the specific discipline or that lack subject-specific nuances. Only adopt definitions or explanations if they align with the academic requirements of your course. - AI-supported learning tools can heavily structure the learning process, for example through predefined learning paths, automatic revision or recommended content. While this can be helpful, it also entails the risk that your learning process becomes overly externally directed: learning is individual and not every recommendation generated by AI suits your personal learning style. Therefore, consciously set your own priorities. - A common risk factor is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: AI in exam preparation
reranked.text:
AI tools can support exam preparation when used in a targeted and reflective manner. They are not a substitute for independent learning, but they can help to structure, deepen and reflect on the learning process.

AI tools can be particularly helpful in organising and supporting the learning process. This includes, among other things:

- Reflection on your own time management
- Support in organising learning activities and setting priorities
- Creating and adapting study and revision plans
- Motivation through structured learning steps or gamification elements
- Revising content, for example through questions or study plans on specific topics
- Gaining an overview of key terms, concepts or subject areas

This means that AI can help to structure the learning process, but it does not replace the need to engage with the subject matter.

AI tools can support learning in various ways, especially when used specifically for organisation, repetition, feedback and self-assessment. The following examples show typical usage scenarios that students can usefully integrate into their exam preparation.

reranked.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
reranked.text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

reranked.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
reranked.text: General information - Do not use generative AI for research - Do not trust AI-generated content; check everything - Write specific prompts with a clear task, context, and details on format and style - Do not rely on AI-generated summaries - Process AI output further - For each course, check the information in u:find, on Moodle, and information on specific assignments and examinations, and clarify any open questions about the use of AI with the respective lecturer Privacy Policy & Terms of Use - Do not enter sensitive or confidential data into AI tools - Anonymise or avoid entering personal data. - Obtain the express consent of third parties before processing their data in an AI. - Ensure that the data you enter is not used for AI training - Only use GDPR-compliant AI tools that do not store data on servers outside the EU. Copyright - Check whether AI-generated texts are based on copyrighted content and cite the authors appropriately - Ensure that copyrighted texts are reproduced correctly. - AI does not have copyright to its own output. Nevertheless, you may not pass off the content as your own. - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own

reranked.title: Citing and labelling AI
reranked.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

reranked.title: Using AI in Your Studies
reranked.text: and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was used in particular to shorten extensive texts and to simplify language, making content easier to understand and more accessible. The AI was used as a supporting tool, while content responsibility and quality review remained with the authors. The use of AI is based on the [Guidelines on AI in Studies and Teaching](https://doi.org/10.25365/phaidra.544), which aim to ensure a responsible and transparent use of AI applications in an academic context.

selected.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
heading_path: This is usually prohibited:
selected.text:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

selected.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
selected.text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

selected.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
selected.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

selected.title: AI in your Studies: What is permitted?
selected.text:
AI can provide support at various points throughout your degree programme, such as idea structuring, understanding complex content and text revision. However, there are clear guidelines in place regarding the application and extent to which AI can be utilised in degree programmes.

The University of Vienna supports the responsible use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes, provided that such use is transparent, complies with regulations, upholds the [principles of academic integrity](https://backend.univie.ac.at/?id=225768), and that it supports you in achieving the intended learning outcomes.

Whether and how the use of AI is permitted always depends on the specific context. In case of doubt, the following applies: AI is not permitted unless clear permission has been granted.

[### Basic framework](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/)

[### What is permitted? And what is not?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/what-is-permitted-and-what-is-not/)

[### Data protection and copyright](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/)

[## Things AI is not recommended for](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/things-ai-is-not-recommended-for/)

[### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/)

selected.title: Basic framework for AI use
selected.text:
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

selected.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Always follow the teacher's instructions!
selected.text: In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

assemble.context:
[1] What is permitted? And what is not? — This is usually prohibited:
The use of AI is prohibited in cases where it undermines the required competencies or where its use has not been disclosed. It is therefore prohibited to

- submit AI-generated texts or solutions as your own work
- allow AI to complete assignments, analyses or arguments in full or to a substantial extent
- use AI in examinations or other forms of assessment where this has not expressly been permitted
- adopt content generated by AI without indicating this where transparency is required
- use fabricated sources, quotations or bibliographies without verification

Such use may be regarded as a breach of the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) or as cheating.

[2] What is permitted? And what is not?
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

[3] AI and Exams — Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

[4] AI in your Studies: What is permitted?
AI can provide support at various points throughout your degree programme, such as idea structuring, understanding complex content and text revision. However, there are clear guidelines in place regarding the application and extent to which AI can be utilised in degree programmes.

The University of Vienna supports the responsible use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes, provided that such use is transparent, complies with regulations, upholds the [principles of academic integrity](https://backend.univie.ac.at/?id=225768), and that it supports you in achieving the intended learning outcomes.

Whether and how the use of AI is permitted always depends on the specific context. In case of doubt, the following applies: AI is not permitted unless clear permission has been granted.

[### Basic framework](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/)

[### What is permitted? And what is not?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/what-is-permitted-and-what-is-not/)

[### Data protection and copyright](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/)

[## Things AI is not recommended for](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/things-ai-is-not-recommended-for/)

[### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/)

[5] Basic framework for AI use
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

[6] AI in academic writing — Use Cases of AI in scientific writing > Always follow the teacher's instructions!
In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

prompt_messages.content:
You are a concise assistant for prospective and current University of Vienna students. Answer the question using ONLY the numbered sources provided.
Rules:
- Base every statement on the sources; never add outside knowledge or guess.
- Cite each claim inline with the bracket number(s) of the source(s) you used, e.g. [1] or [2][3]. Cite only sources you actually relied on.
- If the sources do not contain the answer, reply that you don't know, and do NOT cite any sources.
- Keep the answer short and direct (it may be read aloud by a voice assistant).

Question: Could I be excluded from my studies for using AI in an exam when it wasn't allowed?

generate.raw_response: Yes, you could be excluded from your studies for up to two semesters if you use unauthorized AI tools in an exam [3].

answer: Yes, you could be excluded from your studies for up to two semesters if you use unauthorized AI tools in an exam [3].

citations.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
